data.title: Tesla ordered by Florida jury to pay $329 million in fatal Autopilot crash
data.description:
Key points:
  • One died, one injured in Model S crash
  • Musk has sought to expand autonomous driving
  • Tesla not available for comment

A Florida jury on Friday found Tesla liable in the 2019 fatal crash of an Autopilot-equipped Model S and ordered Elon Musk's automaker to pay $329 million to the family of a deceased woman and an injured survivor.

Jurors in Miami federal court ordered Tesla to pay $129 million in compensatory damages and $200 million in punitive damages to the estate of Naibel Benavides Leon and to her former boyfriend Dillon Angulo.

Lawyers for the plaintiffs said the trial was the first involving the wrongful death of a third party resulting from Autopilot. The plaintiffs had sought $345 million.

Tesla has faced many similar lawsuits over its vehicles' self-driving capabilities, but they have been resolved or dismissed without getting to trial. A judge rejected Tesla's efforts to dismiss the case earlier in the summer , and experts said this may encourage other litigants against the EV maker.

“I think it's a big deal," said Alex Lemann, a professor at Marquette University Law School, who said this may make future settlements more expensive for Tesla. "This is the first time that Tesla has been hit with a judgment in one of the many, many fatalities that have happened as a result of its auto-pilot technology."

Friday's verdict could impede efforts by Musk, the world's richest person, to convince investors that Tesla can become a leader in so-called autonomous driving for private vehicles as well as robotaxis it plans to start producing next year. Shares fell 1.8% on Friday.

Tesla plans to appeal, according to published reports. The Austin, Texas-based company and its lawyers did not immediately respond to several requests for comment.

The trial concerned an April 25, 2019 incident where George McGee drove his 2019 Model S at about 62 mph (100 kph) through an intersection into the victims' parked Chevrolet Tahoe as they were standing beside it on a shoulder.

McGee had reached down to pick up a cellphone he dropped on his car's floorboard and allegedly received no alerts as he ran a stop sign and stop light before hitting the victims' SUV.

"We have a driver who was acting less than perfectly, and yet the jury still found Tesla contributed to the crash," said Philip Koopman, a Carnegie Mellon University engineering professor and expert in autonomous technology. "The only way the jury could have possibly ruled against Tesla was by finding a defect with the Autopilot software. That's a big deal."

Benavides Leon was allegedly thrown 75 feet to her death, while Angulo suffered serious injuries.

"Tesla designed Autopilot only for controlled-access highways yet deliberately chose not to restrict drivers from using it elsewhere, alongside Elon Musk telling the world Autopilot drove better than humans," Brett Schreiber, a lawyer for the plaintiffs, said in a statement.

"Today's verdict represents justice for Naibel's tragic death and Dillon's lifelong injuries," he added.

Last month, Tesla posted its biggest quarterly sales decline in more than a decade, and profit fell short of Wall Street forecasts.

data.title: Investors Seek Safety During Stock Selloff — WSJ
data.description:
By Krystal Hur

Traders searched for cover Friday, with stocks tumbling following a softer-than-expected jobs report and Trump's latest tariff release.

The ratio of put options to call options rose Friday to its highest level since mid-June. That suggests people are reaching for puts, which offer the right to sell a stock at a certain price, to brace for more declines.

Still, the ratio remained well below where it spiked during April's tariff-driven market rout.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Don't Buy Luxury Furniture. Invest in Reupholstery Instead. — WSJ
data.description:
By Sarah Karnasiewicz

In the age of mass-produced "fast furniture," you're lucky if you can find, say, an armchair that's well-built and stylish, or stylish and wallet-friendly. Rarely, if ever, said the pros we polled for this story, will you hit the bull's-eye on all three.

If you want a new sofa from a heritage brand like Baker or George Smith that still handcrafts its wares from quality materials, expect to pay at least $6,000 and more often double or triple that.

Meanwhile bargains tend to skew basic. You might be able to nab a trendy bobbin-leg armchair on Wayfair for $285, but at a price like that, you'll probably have to settle for routine, beige polyester cushions.

You can, however, avoid both boring and budget-busting design with a workaround: Reupholstery. A key decor tool, recovering furniture with new fabric is among the simplest, yet most dramatic upgrades — whether the piece in question is a sturdy but stuffy piece you already own or a bland new buy that just needs personality.

That said, the process can overwhelm the uninitiated. To help demystify it, we asked designers for their top tips.

How do I know what's worth reupholstering?

Remember, value is relative. Pieces from the big-name mass-market retailers aren't what they were even 10-20 years ago, says Atlanta designer Lauren Lowe. That's why it's worth scouring estate sales, auctions or resale sites like Facebook Marketplace for secondhand sofas made by quality companies like Baker and George Smith, and having them recovered to suit your taste.

While fabric and labor won't be cheap, a $4,000 to $5,000 investment could ultimately yield the sort of heirloom-quality sofa that sells for $15,000 at retail.

At the other end of the spectrum, New York designer Jennifer Hunter suggests using new fabric to give generic budget items extra oomph. "Say you find a great silhouette at CB2 for $300 that only needs 2 yards of fabric," she said. "Recovering is a way to get a piece no one else will have."

Designer Lizzie Bailey of Story Street Studio took that tack in a recent home-office overhaul in Westchester County, N.Y. By opting for stock chairs whose cushions can be easily recovered, she said, you can give a space a tailored look without the sticker shock and long lead times of fully custom furniture.

Sentimental value can also justify the effort. A client of Marblehead, Mass., designer Jen Dulac inherited a beloved armchair from her grandmother — only to have it shredded by her house cat. Dulac recovered the piece in a Barclay Butera paisley and reused the original down seat to give it that "gather-you-up-in-a-hug feeling." Now in its second act, the chair has become a favorite reading spot.

Where do I find an upholsterer?

This can be tricky. Upholstery is a dying art and the remaining companies tend to be local, family-run and off the radar. "If they have an Instagram account, they're probably pricey or trade only," cautioned Hunter. Best bet: Rely on word-of-mouth referrals or an online equivalent, like Yelp reviews. While designers often guard their sources ferociously, Lowe suggests asking antique stores, which frequently get items recovered, for recommendations. For smaller, shippable jobs, like cushions, consider craftspeople on Etsy.

How much will it cost?

Prices can vary widely depending on the cost of fabric, the scope and complexity of the job and even where the work is done. "I know some designers who actually send pieces to Texas to be recovered because even with freight, it's cheaper than doing it in New York City," said Hunter.

According to 2025 data from Angie's List, most projects fall between $370 and $1,145. Accent chairs might be in the $200-$1000 range, while sofas generally run into the low to mid thousands.

What about fabric?

First consider how the item will be used: Is it an occasional perch or a sofa that doubles as a climbing gym? "Martindale rating" refers to a fabric's durability; generally, you want a rating of 15,000- 20,000 or above. Lowe says that heavyweight linen is always a stylish and sturdy choice. Nervous about spills? "You can make almost any fabric 'performance' by having it backed or treated with stain guard," said Hunter.

Got any secret tricks?

That Schumacher ikat you've got your eye on? We're sorry to report it costs $207 a yard — and a typical couch requires around 16. Happily, says Hunter, there are plenty of other ways to give furniture a "designer" look without dropping a ton of coin. For instance, recover most of a piece in an inexpensive solid but splurge on contrast fabric for the back of a chair or piping for a couch. "There's so much you can do on a shoestring," she said. "You just need the vision."

data.title: Airsculpt Technologies, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Airsculpt Technologies, Inc., a leading provider of body contouring procedures, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report highlights the company's financial performance and operational activities amidst a challenging market environment in the aesthetics industry.

Financial Highlights

Revenue: Airsculpt Technologies reported revenue of $44.0 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, marking a 13.7% decrease compared to the same period in 2024. For the six months ended June 30, 2025, revenue was $83.4 million, a 15.5% decline from the same period in 2024.

Income/(loss) from operations: The company achieved an income from operations of $0.8 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to a loss of $5.0 million for the same period in 2024. However, for the six months ended June 30, 2025, the company reported a loss from operations of $(0.8) million, compared to an income of $6.0 million for the same period in 2024.

Net (loss)/income: Airsculpt Technologies recorded a net loss of $(0.6) million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, an improvement from the net loss of $(3.2) million for the same period in 2024. For the six months ended June 30, 2025, the net loss was $(3.4) million, compared to a net income of $2.8 million for the same period in 2024.

(Loss)/income per share of common stock - basic and diluted: The loss per share was $(0.01) for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $(0.06) for the same period in 2024. For the six months ended June 30, 2025, the loss per share was $(0.06), compared to an income per share of $0.05 for the same period in 2024.

Business Highlights

Sales Units: Airsculpt performed 3,392 cases in the three months ended June 30, 2025, and 6,468 cases in the six months ended June 30, 2025. This represents a decline from 3,949 and 7,695 cases for the same periods in 2024, respectively.

Revenue Segments: The company operates a single reportable segment focused on direct medical procedure services, specifically the AirSculpt® body contouring procedures.

Geographical Performance: As of August 1, 2025, AirSculpt operates 32 centers across 20 states in the U.S., as well as in Canada and the United Kingdom, highlighting its extensive nationwide and international footprint.

New Product Launches: The company is piloting a standalone skin tightening procedure, aiming to leverage its existing infrastructure and expertise to generate incremental revenues.

Future Outlook: AirSculpt is focusing on stabilizing revenue growth through strategic priorities such as optimizing marketing investments, improving sales strategies, enhancing customer experience, expanding consumer financing offerings, and introducing new services.

Operational Strategy: The company has implemented a cost reduction program estimated to save approximately $3.0 million annually and has paused new center and procedure room openings to focus on optimizing existing operations.

SEC Filing: Airsculpt Technologies, Inc. [ AIRS ] - 10-Q - Aug. 01, 2025

data.title: Mexico Manufacturing Indexes Mixed in July — Market Talk
data.description: Purchasing managers indexes for Mexico were mixed in July while pointing to continued contraction. S&P Global's PMI rose to 49.1 from 46.3 in June, while the Inegi/Bank of Mexico index fell 0.9 of a point, to 49.8, and the Mexican Institute of Finance Executives' manufacturing index fell 2.1 points, to 45.5. "Mexico's manufacturing downturn appears to be easing, with a more moderate decline in new orders helping to slow the reduction in production volumes during July," S&P says. Panel members attributed the drop in new orders to U.S. tariffs and the entry of Chinese products into the domestic market exacerbating competitive pressures, S&P adds. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1554 ET - Purchasing managers indexes for Mexico were mixed in July while pointing to continued contraction. S&P Global's PMI rose to 49.1 from 46.3 in June, while the Inegi/Bank of Mexico index fell 0.9 of a point, to 49.8, and the Mexican Institute of Finance Executives' manufacturing index fell 2.1 points, to 45.5. "Mexico's manufacturing downturn appears to be easing, with a more moderate decline in new orders helping to slow the reduction in production volumes during July," S&P says. Panel members attributed the drop in new orders to U.S. tariffs and the entry of Chinese products into the domestic market exacerbating competitive pressures, S&P adds. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1542 ET - 1541 - Treasury yields plunge amid dismal U.S. jobs numbers and increasing pressure on the Fed to cut interest rates. Job creation in July is lower-than-expected while previous monthly readings are drastically trimmed by the BLS. Trump threatens to fire the agency's head, Erika McEntarfer, a Biden appointee the president accuses of doctoring the data. Next week will be relatively light on data points and Fedspeak. The 10-year falls 0.142 percentage point to 4.218%, its biggest daily decline since April. It's down 0.167 p.p. for the week. The two-year loses 0.250 p.p. to 3.702%, largest one-day fall in a year, and is down 0.214 p.p. this week. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1540 ET - U.S. natural gas futures settle a couple of cents lower in range-bound trade, holding above the key $3 level with support from some warmer weather seen later this month and a pickup in LNG feedgas flows. "The bearishness seemed to hit exhaustion levels and the traders that have a target of $3 to take profit apparently did so," Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group says in a note. "It sure helped that we got a little bit of heat as well." Nymex natural gas settles down 0.7% at $3.083/mmBtu, for a 2.4% weekly loss. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1539 ET - Mexico received $5.2 billion in remittances in June, down 16% from the year-earlier month in a third consecutive decline, Bank of Mexico data show. Remittances in the first half of the year were down 5.6%, at $29.6 billion. "The flow of remittances to Mexico has experienced a streak of 11 consecutive years of growth, which will most likely end in 2025," BBVA's Juan Jose Li Ng says in a note. A high base of comparison is one of the main factors explaining the double-digit declines in April and June of this year, he says. "Recent U.S. government immigration actions and policies are only marginally affecting the decline in remittances to Mexico, which have shown signs of uncertainty over the past 20 months, a trend that began to be observed in November 2023." (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1519 ET - Most-active live cattle futures and lean hog futures on the CME finished the day on the positive side. Live cattle closed up 0.2% to $2.246 a pound, and lean hog futures closed up 0.6% to 90.175 cents a pound. For both, it extends a winning trend that started in late July, continuing to the first day of August. With today's close, both cattle and hog futures have closed higher for four of the past five trading sessions, according to FactSet data. It was a notable month for cattle, setting record-highs multiple times, including a new all-time high of $2.30 a pound hit Wednesday. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1438 ET - The U.S. dollar fell after President Trump announced new tariffs and July payrolls were weaker than expected, while jobs numbers for May and June were revised sharply lower. But even being down by 0.8%, the U.S. dollar hasn't boosted most grain futures. A lower U.S. dollar makes export sales of U.S. grains more competitive on the world stage. Grain futures have been pressured by a wave of fresh tariffs, pumping more uncertainty into the import/export business. The President still has time next week to amend tariffs, but analysts and investors are unsure if he will. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Fed's Kugler resigning from Fed effective Aug 8
data.description:
The Federal Reserve said Friday that Governor Adriana Kugler is resigning early from her term and will exit the central bank on Aug. 8, potentially shaking up what was already a fractious succession process for Fed leadership amid difficult relations with President Donald Trump.

The Fed said in a statement that Kugler, who became a governor in September 2023, will leave before her term’s conclusion, which was scheduled for Jan. 31, 2026. In a press release, the Fed said Kugler will return to Georgetown University as a professor next autumn.

Kugler did not attend this week’s rate-setting Federal Open Market Committee meeting, which is unusual.

Kugler’s early exit may shake up the timeline for the succession process now surrounding Chair Jerome Powell, whose term ends next May. Trump has threatened to fire Powell repeatedly believing interest rates should be much lower than they are.

Trump will now get to select a Fed governor to replace Kugler and finish out her term. Some speculation had centered on the idea Trump might pick a potential future chair to fill that slot. The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment about the Fed appointment.

In a letter to Trump announcing her resignation, Kugler wrote “I am proud to have tackled this role with integrity, a strong commitment to serving the public, and with a data-driven approach strongly based on my expertise in labor markets and inflation.”

Kugler’s time at the Fed was a challenging one as central bankers raised rates aggressively to combat high inflation pressures. Those high rates have put them in the crosshairs of Trump and have caused economic challenges, although inflation pressures have moved much closer to the 2% target.

data.title: What Companies Have Said About Jobs in the Past Quarter — WSJ
data.description:
By Inti Pacheco

Even before today's dismal jobs report, companies have been sounding off on layoffs and slower hiring in recent weeks.

On Thursday, Moderna executives said the biotech company would reduce its headcount by the end of the year to under 5,000, down from 5,800 at the beginning of this year.

Procter & Gamble announced in June that it would cut 15% of its non-manufacturing workforce, or up to 7,000 roles, part of a push to create smaller and broader teams. In July, Microsoft said it is cutting 9,000 jobs, or about 4% of its global workforce, in its latest round of layoffs.

Likewise, Intel is cutting 15% of its workforce. Most of the layoffs, which began in the first few weeks of the current quarter, targeted middle management, the company said.

An even greater number of companies are simply slowing or freezing hiring. In earnings calls over the past two weeks, companies in the business of helping employers recruit and bring aboard new employees say that reticence is showing up in their own results.

At temporary-staffing agency ManpowerGroup, U.S. revenue declined 3% in the second quarter, driven by a decline in its Experis business, which focuses on finding IT talent for other companies. Equifax, meanwhile, is feeling the slowdown in its business helping employers do background checks and bring on new hires.

"Overall, U.S. hiring and particularly white-collar hiring, continued to be relatively weak in the second quarter," said Equifax Chief Executive Mark Begor last week during a call with analysts.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Tesla Will Pay $329 Million in Damages for Fatal Autopilot Crash, Jury Says — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mariapaula Gonzalez

Tesla will pay $329 million in damages for a fatal 2019 crash involving its Autopilot software, a Miami jury determined Friday.

The automaker's costs include $200 million in punitive damages and $129 million in compensatory damages, according to multiple media reports. The crash killed a 22-year-old woman and severely injured her partner.

The jury determined that Tesla was 33% responsible for the crash, and blamed the driver for the remainder, the New York Times reported.

This is breaking news. Check back for more updates.

data.title: Federal Reserve Gov. Adriana Kugler to Resign
data.description:
By Matt Grossman

Federal Reserve Gov. Adriana Kugler plans to resign from her post Aug. 8, she wrote Friday in a letter to President Trump.

Kugler's term as a governor was set to expire early next year. The vacant board seat opens a slot for Trump to nominate an eventual successor to Fed Chair Jerome Powell to join the Fed's board, should he choose an external candidate to succeed Powell. That person could then be nominated to succeed Powell as Fed chair after Powell's chair term expires in May.

Kugler was nominated to the Fed board by former President Joe Biden and took office in 2023. She was absent from the Fed's meeting this week. Throughout her time on the board she voted in line with its majority decisions on monetary policy.

She plans to return to Georgetown University as a professor in the fall, the Fed said.

data.title: Global stock index sinks with dollar, bond yields after weak US jobs data
data.description:
Key points:
  • Equities sink, dollar and Treasury yields tumble
  • US July nonfarm payrolls weaker than expected, June sharply lower
  • US announces new tariff rates ahead of trade talks deadline
  • Oil prices in steep decline on supply boost worries

MSCI's global equities index sold off sharply on Friday and the dollar took a dive after weaker-than-expected U.S. jobs data ramped up expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts in September while investors also considered U.S. President Donald Trump's latest tariff announcements.

U.S. Treasuries were in demand after the Labor Department reported that the U.S. economy added 73,000 nonfarm payrolls last month, below the 110,000 expected by economists surveyed by Reuters. July unemployment rose to 4.2%. June's job growth was revised sharply lower to 14,000 from 147,000.

After the report, Trump said on Friday afternoon that he ordered his team to fire the commissioner of the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Erika L. McEntarfer, nominated by prior President Joe Biden for the role.

After the data, traders were betting on a 69% probability for a September rate cut compared with 37.7% on Thursday, according to CME Group's FedWatch tool.

"The market is reacting to the possibility of the economy flipping into recession. The weak jobs data is piling on to weak earnings reports and weak guidance from some corporations," said Luke Tilley, Chief Economist, Wilmington Trust.

MSCI's gauge of stocks across the globe EURONEXT:IACWI fell 11.89 points, or 1.27%, to 917.80, putting it on track for its biggest daily drop since late April. The softer jobs data added to losses for the global index, which was already in the red after a host of tariff announcements from Trump the day before.

In time for an August 1 deadline, Trump ordered tariffs ranging from 10% to 41% on U.S. imports from several major trading partners. He increased duties on Canadian goods to 35% from 25% for all products not covered by the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade agreement. He set a 25% rate for India's U.S.-bound exports, 20% for Taiwan's, 19% for Thailand's and 15% for South Korea's. However, Mexico got a 90-day reprieve from higher tariffs to allow for deal talks.

"We've had some mixed earnings from the big companies. We've had a big run higher in the market. We've had broad tariffs implemented and a labor market report that was softer than expected," Scott Wren, senior global market strategist, Wells Fargo Investment Institute. "All of those things have played into today's sell-off."

On Wall Street at 2:33 p.m. ET the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 529.87 points, or 1.20%, to 43,601.24, the S&P 500 fell 99.53 points, or 1.57%, to 6,240.00 and the Nasdaq Composite fell 456.71 points, or 2.16%, to 20,666.45.

Earlier the pan-European STOXX 600 index ended down 1.89%, its biggest daily drop since April 9.

In currencies, the greenback reversed course to fall sharply after the data due to increased expectations for rate cuts. Earlier it had found support in fading hopes for U.S. rate cuts.

The dollar index , which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, fell 1.02% to 99.01.

The euro up 1.1% at $1.1541 while sterling strengthened 0.23% to $1.3236 and the Canadian dollar strengthened 0.43% versus the greenback to C$1.38 per dollar.

Against the Japanese yen , the dollar weakened 1.98% to 147.75. The Bank of Japan held interest rates steady on Thursday and revised up its near-term inflation expectations, but Governor Kazuo Ueda sounded a little dovish in the press conference.

U.S. Treasury yields plunged after the jobs data and the increase in bets that the Fed will resume interest rate cuts in September.

The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes fell 13.2 basis points to 4.228%, from 4.36% late on Thursday while the 30-year bond (US30YT=RR) yield fell 7.4 basis points to 4.8111%.

The 2-year note (US2YT=RR) yield, which typically moves in step with interest rate expectations for the Federal Reserve, fell 23.7 basis points to 3.715%, from 3.951% late on Thursday.

In energy markets , oil prices sank about 2.8% after the jobs data and on jitters about a possible production increase by OPEC and its allies. Oil had settled around 1% lower on Thursday.

U.S. crude settled down 2.79%, or $1.93 at $67.33 a barrel and Brent settled at $69.67 per barrel, down 2.83%, or $2.03 on the day.

Elsewhere in commodities, gold prices rallied to a one-week high, after the weak jobs report boosted policy easing expectations and the latest tariff announcements spurred safe-haven demand.

Spot gold rose 1.78% to $3,348.72 an ounce.

data.title: Wheat Futures Fall as Traders Set Aside Weaker Dollar — Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Wheat for September delivery fell 1.2% to $5.16 1/2 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade on Friday, falling despite the weaker dollar that tends to buoy futures, as good weather and the apparent implementation of new tariffs leave traders risk-off.
  • Corn for December delivery fell 0.7% to $4.10 3/4 a bushel.
  • Soybeans for November delivery fell 0.1% to $9.88 1/2 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Pressing Forward: The U.S. dollar fell after President Trump announced new tariffs and July payrolls were weaker than expected, while jobs numbers for May and June were revised sharply lower.

But even being down by 0.8%, the U.S. dollar did little to nothing for grain futures. A lower U.S. dollar makes export sales of U.S. grains more competitive on the world stage, but grains instead were pressured by a wave of fresh tariffs, pumping more uncertainty into the import/export business.

The president still has time next week to amend tariffs, but analysts and investors are unsure if he will.

Taking a Drink: Soil moisture for Argentine crops is improving thanks to timely rains, according to the Buenos Aires Grain Exchange. This is particularly true for Argentina's wheat, which put pressure on Chicago futures.

With the wheat crop condition in Argentina over 95% excellent, the country is seen as a strong competitor to the U.S., with fresh tariffs unsettling existing supply chain norms for grains and potentially giving Argentina more opportunity to sell grain exports.

Not Quite There: CBOT soybean futures were positive for much of the day, but ultimately unable to end the losing streak seen in soybeans across the past five trading sessions, and eight of the past nine trading days.

Soybeans fell under the $10/bushel mark and shed nearly 10% of their value in that time, as fund traders have taken an increasingly negative view on the prospects for futures, Matt Zeller of StoneX said in a note.

INSIGHT

Lacking Spark: The USDA reported a new round of flash sales of U.S. corn exports, with two sales reported to unknown destinations for delivery in the 2025-26 marketing year. A total of 352,160 metric tons of corn were sold, according to the USDA.

Export demand has been increasingly a major driver for CBOT grain futures, but this sale didn't push corn into positive territory.

"The market continues to be weighed down by the large U.S. crop on the way," John Stewart and Associates said in a note.

Light Drought: U.S. crops managed to make it out of June and July mostly unscathed, with the U.S. Drought Monitor reporting shrinking drought coverage of farmland across the country this week.

Of Corn Belt states, only North Dakota and Nebraska are seeing any large areas affected by dryness, while for the rest of the Corn Belt dryness it is practically nonexistent, making a record-size crop forecast by the USDA this year more likely.

"A year ago heat in the second half of August and rapid net soil moisture loss stole from potential from final yields," said Daniel Flynn of Price Futures Group in a note.

AHEAD

  • Tyson Foods Inc. is scheduled to release its 3Q 2025 earnings report at 7:30 a.m. EDT Monday.
  • The USDA is due to release its weekly grain export inspections report at 11 a.m. EDT Monday.
  • The USDA is scheduled to release its weekly crop progress report at 4 p.m. EDT Monday.
  • Andersons Inc. is scheduled to release its 2Q 2025 earnings report at 4:05 p.m. EDT Monday.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

Even more suprising, revisions to May and June's data revised jobs down by a huge 258,000, ING analysts say in a note. This highlights that the jobs market has lost momentum even earlier than thought and will intensify pressure from President Trump for the Federal Reserve to loosen monetary policy, ING says. With tariffs set to eat into household spending and corporate profits, the case for cutting interest rates has been significantly strengthened, ING says. Lower interest rates typically benefit non-interest bearing bullion. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1036 ET - The European Central Bank will likely next move to cut interest rates in December, given weaker growth in the second half and softer inflation after some resilience in current activity, Barclays economist Mariano Cena says in a note. By December, there should be clear signals on trade headwinds, reduced concerns about the impact of supply-chain disruptions on inflation, and increased comfort that fiscal plans for 2026 won't reignite inflation pressures, he says. That supports a final 25 basis-point "insurance" cut. Still, risks of a persistent inflation undershoot suggest a need for policy flexibility toward the end of the year, Cena notes. Barclays earlier this week removed its previous expectation of an ECB rate cut in September. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

1031 ET - Uncertainty surrounding U.S. tariffs remains, Societe Generale's Wei Yao says in a note. The U.S. announced new tariffs on Canada, Switzerland, Taiwan, and other trade partners, with most delayed until August 7. "It is tempting to think that a large part of the tariff uncertainty is behind us now, but we remain cautious," Yao says. The current trade agreements are vague on details, she says. "Much confusion and many questions linger over all the investment commitments." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1005 ET - U.S. crops managed to make it out of June and July mostly unscathed, with the U.S. Drought Monitor reporting shrinking drought coverage of farmland across the country this week. Of Corn Belt states, only North Dakota and Nebraska are seeing any large areas affected by dryness, while for the rest of the Corn Belt dryness its practically nonexistent--making a record-sized crops forecast by the USDA this year more likely. "A year ago heat in the second half of August and rapid net soil moisture loss stole from potential from final yields," says Daniel Flynn of Price Futures Group in a note. CBOT corn falls 0.5%, soybeans slide 0.2%, and wheat is down 0.9%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0952 ET - Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack says she remains comfortable with the central bank's choice to hold rates steady this week even after seeing weakening job creation in the July jobs report Friday morning. In a TV interview with Bloomberg, Hammack says the Fed is right to be attentive to inflation, too. "We have to look at, if we're missing, by how much and for how long do we expect those misses to last? And right now, we're missing by much more on the inflation side than we're missing on the employment side." Hammack isn't a Fed voter this year. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)
